channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: The 9 Hard Truths Killing AI Products Before They Ship
executive_summary: Nate B Jones argues that current chat models are only a weakly intelligent starting point for AI tasks, as they lack real data integration and can only initiate tasks. To unlock real value, we need to focus on multi-turn conversations, better planning, and seamless data access, while recognizing that tool ecosystems are diversifying, much like the rivalry between Mac and Windows.
bullets: Chat models are described as a 'weakly intelligent layer' that can start tasks but not finish them. This means they can initiate a conversation but lack the depth needed to complete complex tasks. It's like a car that can start but can't drive to the destination.

Real value in AI emerges from 'multi-turn conversations' where insights are refined over multiple exchanges. This approach treats the first message as an anchor, allowing subsequent messages to build on it. Think of it like a relay race, where each runner builds on the momentum of the last.

Conversations should be the primary artifact of computing, not files. This means we need tools that prioritize dialogue over traditional data storage, making it easier for users to interact with AI. It's like cooking where the recipe is less important than the ingredients you have on hand.

Doubling the time spent on planning an AI session can yield 'outsized gains' because AI can amplify good plans and magnify bad ones. This means that careful preparation can lead to significantly better outcomes. Imagine planting a garden; if you take the time to prepare the soil, your plants will thrive.

Tool ecosystems like Cursor, Claude Code, and Lovable are splintering, reflecting different skill levels and brand preferences. Choosing a tool now is similar to picking between Mac and Windows, where your choice can define your work style and capabilities. It's like choosing a sports team; each has its strengths and fan base.

Token depth is crucial for agent value, as agents use more tokens to perform tasks on behalf of users. Providing users with control over their token usage can unlock more complex problems. Think of it like a subscription service where you pay for the level of access you need.

Data middleware is identified as the 'missing middle' in AI development, as many organizations are restricting data access. Startups that can create seamless data flows will gain a competitive edge. It's like a highway system; if you can build the right roads, traffic will flow smoothly.

The current approach to AI assumes one-shot prompts and file-based workflows, which limits the potential of AI. We need to shift our mindset to treat conversations as the fundamental unit of computing. It's like trying to build a house with only a single brick instead of a full set of building materials.

The speaker emphasizes that 'the leverage isn’t in the model; it’s in how well you plan the conversation before you ever hit ‘send.’ This highlights the importance of strategic thinking in AI interactions. Imagine a chess game where each move is carefully calculated to outsmart your opponent.

AI products are failing to ship because of a lack of focus on conversation design and planning. Without addressing these issues, teams risk launching ineffective tools. It's like a chef serving a dish without tasting it first; the result may be disappointing.

By the end of the year, organizations need to prioritize creating tools that support conversational computing. If they don't, they risk falling behind in the rapidly evolving AI landscape. Think of it like a race; if you don't start training now, you won't be ready when the competition begins.

The bottleneck in AI development isn't the quality of models but the availability of data. Companies that can secure and manage data effectively will lead the market. It's like having a well-stocked pantry; without the right ingredients, you can't cook a great meal.
key_takeaways: Focus on multi-turn conversations when designing AI interactions. This approach allows for deeper insights and better outcomes. If you ignore this, you risk creating tools that only scratch the surface of user needs.

Invest time in planning your AI sessions, aiming to double the planning time. This can lead to significantly better results, as AI can amplify good plans. Skipping this step may lead to wasted resources and ineffective AI solutions.

By the end of the year, prioritize the development of tools that make conversations the primary unit of computing. This shift is crucial for staying competitive in the AI landscape. If you delay, you may find your products are outdated before they even launch.

technical_terms.weakly intelligent layer: A type of AI that can start tasks but lacks the ability to complete them effectively. Think of it like a bicycle that can get you moving but can't take you far without pedaling.
technical_terms.multi-turn conversation: A dialogue that involves multiple exchanges, allowing for deeper exploration of ideas and insights. Like a game of catch, where each throw builds on the last to improve the play.
technical_terms.conversational computing: A computing paradigm where conversations are the main focus, rather than traditional file-based interactions. Think of it like a conversation at a dinner table, where the discussion is more important than the menu.
technical_terms.token depth: The amount of tokens used by AI agents to perform tasks, which determines their effectiveness. Like a bank account where the more you deposit, the more you can withdraw.
technical_terms.data middleware: A layer that facilitates the flow of data between systems, making it easier to access and use. Think of it like a bridge that connects two islands, allowing for easy travel between them.
technical_terms.tool ecosystems: A collection of tools that serve different purposes and user needs, often reflecting varying levels of expertise. Like a toolbox where each tool has a specific function, catering to different types of projects.
technical_terms.planning: The process of structuring an AI session to maximize effectiveness and outcomes. Like drafting a blueprint before building a house, ensuring everything is in place before construction begins.
technical_terms.agents: AI systems that perform tasks on behalf of users, often using tokens to do so. Think of them as personal assistants who handle tasks while you focus on bigger projects.
technical_terms.file-based workflows: Traditional methods of organizing and managing data through files rather than conversations. Like using a filing cabinet instead of a digital cloud, limiting accessibility and collaboration.
technical_terms.conversational artifacts: The outputs of conversations that serve as the main focus of computing tasks. Think of them like the notes taken during a meeting, which capture the essence of the discussion.
